Even within your head, it's unclear.
Is there a face on it? It does seem to be slightly illuminated from within itself.
It's not an outhouse.
good question, maybe @mknepprath knows

A clearing in the gloom. A hut stands ahead with a medallion hanging from its doorframe. To the left, an iron gate leads to a graveyard. A crumbling tower rises to the right. Behind the hut, a dark path descends toward a swamp.
They become more and more receptive to receiving your orders. You reward them with more paste.
You place the flower on the ground and direct the ants to carry it across the room. You reward them with paste.
You don't have that item as it's a bit tricky to pick up.
You don't have a flower in your inventory. The ants would like one, though.
The ant is so well trained,  it scurries over to the key and picks it up for you. You have the key.
I don't know...
You place the hut's door on top of the pile and... close it. The door slides to the ground.
We do have safety concerns about this place.
Tastes a bit bitter. You spit it out.
As you start pulling up the ground around Spookytown, you realize it probably has a bad aftertaste.
Chomp. Broke a tooth.

Suggested moves:
- "look around"
- "pick up A"
- "push B"
The ground feels so close to you.
Murky.
You are in Spookytown.
You spy a totem of some kind in the hut.

Suggested moves:
- "look around"
- "pick up A"
- "push B"
All of your focus is drawn to this medallion. As soon as your fingers wrap around it, you feel energy blast through your body - you are now 40 meters tall.
You may need to pick it up if you want to try to do that.
You're not holding the medallion. Trust me, you want to hold the medallion.
You commune with the totem. It dismisses you.
You transplant it from the ground, but it crumbles in your hands.
blame @mknepprath, idk
It's so small, yet is sending a searing pain through your head - needling you to get that totem below.
In the darkness you catch different colors reflecting off of its surface.
You don't have a coin!
You don't have a coin. No banana for you.
The merchant generously gives you an apple for your bent coin.
This coin is bent! You're not getting a banana for that.
You spy a totem in some kind in the hut.
You see a large sign, a small shop across the way, and an old well.
With your head over the trees, you can see for miles - where the sky turns from grey to blue, Crescent Plaza, and below you: a little totem calling for you from within a hut.
This place is a dump. You did this.
There's a back wall, left wall, right wall, and a front wall with a door. A door!

There's a front wall with bars, back wall, left wall, and right wall. That front wall looks interesting. Something over there catches your attention.

Suggested move:
- "inspect the front wall"
You can open it. You do open it. It is now open.
The coin is now bent.
The coin is now bent. This was a strange thing to do with a coin.
The medallion is yearning for something within the hut's remains. The totem pulses.
A portal opens up around you and you pass through into... Spookytown, 5 minutes ago.
There doesn't seem to be a way through.
It is not of this plane.
You hear yowls in reply to the mere thought.
It is not benevolent, and it doesn't appreciate you considering that.
Honestly, calling it a hut is being generous. It's a crude structure.
You're HUGE, you smash a hole through the top of the hut, which then collapses. Guess you can get at that totem now.
The purpose of this totem is not yet clear. But this ain't it.
There's no fixing that.
Thick, inky nothingness surrounds you.
Spookytown responds to the totem. Spookytown has been angered.
Spookytown responds to the totem.
The grip of the void is loosened and you slip down onto a glassy surface. Your surroundings are still quite foggy, however.
You're welcome!

A crooked iron gate creaks open as you approach. Headstones lean at odd angles, half-swallowed by fog. A single lantern flickers near a freshly turned grave. Beyond the rows of stone, you see a sealed crypt.
You can barely see your hand in front of your face. The swamp is too dark to navigate without a light source.
Your lantern cuts through the murk as you wade into knee-deep water. Gnarled trees claw at the sky. A rickety dock juts out into the blackness, where a hooded figure stands motionless in a boat.
A crumbling stone staircase spirals up through a gap in the thorns. At the top, a watchtower leans against the wind. A faint blue glow pulses from the window above.

Fog clings to everything. Crooked headstones stretch in every direction. A lantern flickers near a grave to your left. Ahead, a stone crypt is sealed with a heavy door. The iron gate behind you leads back to the clearing.
The headstones are unchanged, but the crypt door hangs open now. Darkness spills out from within.
A lantern sits on the ground near a freshly turned grave. The earth here is soft and dark.
More headstones disappearing into the fog. Some have been knocked over. One has deep claw marks scratched into it.
No stars. No moon. Just a low ceiling of grey nothing.
Wet grass and mud. Something white pokes out of the soil near your foot. On closer inspection, it's a small bone. You leave it alone.
You pick up the lantern. Its flame dances but doesn't go out, despite there being no oil inside. Weird.
You pick up the lantern. Its flame dances but doesn't go out, despite there being no oil inside. Weird.
A small iron lantern with no oil reservoir. The flame inside flickers blue at its core. It's warm to the touch.
A small iron lantern with no oil reservoir. The flame inside flickers blue at its core. It's warm to the touch.
The headstone reads: 'Here lies one who wandered far and forgot the way home.' Fresh dirt. No flowers.
The headstone reads: 'Here lies one who wandered far and forgot the way home.' Fresh dirt. No flowers.
'Here lies one who wandered far and forgot the way home.' The letters are carved deep, as if by fingernails.
Most are too weathered to read. One says 'REMEMBERED.' Another: 'SHE TRIED.' A third is blank.
Heavy stone door with an iron lock shaped like a ribcage. There's a keyhole where the heart would be.
Heavy stone door with an iron lock shaped like a ribcage. There's a keyhole where the heart would be.
The door won't budge. The lock holds firm. You'd need some kind of key — something that fits the ribcage.
The bone key slides into the ribcage lock with a soft click. The stone door grinds open, releasing air that hasn't moved in a very long time.
The bone key slides into the ribcage lock with a soft click. The stone door grinds open, releasing air that hasn't moved in a very long time.
The key doesn't fit. The lock is shaped like a ribcage — you need something more... anatomical.
The door is sealed. An iron lock shaped like a ribcage holds it shut.
You step inside. It's small and cold. On a stone pedestal in the center sits a brass compass. Its needle spins lazily, then locks onto something you can't see.
On a stone pedestal sits a brass compass. The needle points somewhere beyond the walls.
You lift the compass from the pedestal. It's heavier than it looks. The needle swings toward you for a moment, then settles on a direction that doesn't exist on any map.
You lift the compass from the pedestal. It's heavier than it looks. The needle swings toward you for a moment, then settles on a direction that doesn't exist on any map.
Brass, tarnished, warm. The glass face is cracked but intact. The needle points somewhere impossible.
You claw at the dirt with your hands. After a while you stop. Whatever is down there wants to stay down there.
You scratch at the earth near the closest grave. The soil is soft but your resolve isn't. You stop.
You punch the stone. The stone wins.
You kick it. Your foot hurts now. The crypt is unimpressed.
You slip back through the iron gate into the clearing.
You slip back through the iron gate into the clearing.
You slip back through the iron gate into the clearing.
You're in an old graveyard. Try: look around, inspect things, take items, read headstones, open the crypt.
A graveyard on the edge of Spookytown. Fog everywhere. Headstones, a lantern, and a sealed crypt.
You call out into the fog. No answer. But you swear the mist shifts, just slightly, in acknowledgment.
You hold the lantern up. The fog retreats a few inches around you. The headstone inscriptions seem sharper in this light.
Dark water stretches in every direction. Your lantern catches the gleam of eyes just below the surface — then they're gone. A hooded figure stands on a dock ahead, waiting beside a small boat.
The dock is empty now. The ferryman has departed. Ripples fan out across the black water where the boat was.
Twisted roots breach the water like grasping fingers. Something glints beneath the surface but it's too deep to reach.
Cattails and silence. A heron watches you from a dead tree, perfectly still.
The canopy is so thick it feels like being underground. Your lantern is the only sky down here.
Murky water. You can see your own dim reflection, but it takes a moment too long to mirror your movements.
The figure doesn't look up. A dry voice: 'I ferry the grieving. Bring me a tear shed by one who cannot cry, and I will give you what you seek.'
A tall figure in a tattered hood. You can't see a face — just shadow where a face should be. Bony hands grip a long pole.
A tall figure in a tattered hood. You can't see a face — just shadow where a face should be. Bony hands grip a long pole.

The ferryman extends a skeletal palm. The tear rolls off your hand and into his. It glows faintly, then absorbs into his bones. 'Passage granted.' He reaches into the boat and offers you a key carved from bone.

The ferryman extends a skeletal palm. The tear rolls off your hand and into his. It glows faintly, then absorbs into his bones. 'Passage granted.' He reaches into the boat and offers you a key carved from bone.

The ferryman extends a skeletal palm. The tear rolls off your hand and into his. It glows faintly, then absorbs into his bones. 'Passage granted.' He reaches into the boat and offers you a key carved from bone.
A flat-bottomed skiff, barely held together by pitch and stubbornness. The ferryman's hand rests on the pole, ready.
A flat-bottomed skiff, barely held together by pitch and stubbornness. The ferryman's hand rests on the pole, ready.
The ferryman holds up a hand. 'Not yet. You haven't paid.'
The ferryman shakes his head slowly. 'I have no use for the currency of the living.'
You dip a foot in. The water is ice cold and something brushes against your ankle. You pull back. Nope.
Black and still. Occasionally a bubble rises to the surface and pops silently. You don't want to think about what's down there.
Black and still. Occasionally a bubble rises to the surface and pops silently. You don't want to think about what's down there.
It stares at you with one ancient eye. You get the feeling it's been here longer than anything else.
It blinks once. That's all you're getting.
You sweep the lantern across the water. For a moment you see shapes moving beneath the surface — long, pale, drifting. Then the water goes dark again.
You wade back through the muck toward the clearing.
You wade back through the muck toward the clearing.
You wade back through the muck toward the clearing.
You're in a dark swamp. Try: look around, talk to the ferryman, inspect things, give items.
A swamp at the edge of Spookytown. A ferryman waits at a dock. The water is not friendly.
A circular room at the top of a crumbling staircase. Moonlight leaks through cracks in the stone. A translucent figure hovers in the center of the room, flickering like a candle. It looks... sad.
The tower room is still. The spirit is gone. Where it floated, a faint warmth remains in the air. Through the window, you can see further than before.
A narrow window. Through it you can see the graveyard far below, lit by a faint glow.
Cracked stone walls. Someone carved tally marks here — hundreds of them.
A hole in the roof. You can see one star through it. Just one.
The staircase spirals into darkness below. You'd rather not think about how high up you are.
You can see the whole of Spookytown from here. The graveyard. The swamp's canopy. The hut clearing. And beyond it all — a void where the horizon should be.

The figure turns toward you. Its mouth moves but the sound arrives late, like an echo in reverse: 'I've forgotten what living things feel like. Something growing. Something real. Do you have anything... alive?'
A wispy, translucent figure. Neither old nor young. Its edges blur and reform constantly. It seems to be looking at something far away, or maybe at nothing at all.
A wispy, translucent figure. Neither old nor young. Its edges blur and reform constantly. It seems to be looking at something far away, or maybe at nothing at all.

The spirit reaches out. As its fingers close around the flower, color floods into the petals — brighter than before. The spirit smiles. A single tear rolls down its face and crystallizes in the air before falling into your palm. 'Thank you,' it whispers, and fades into the moonlight.

The spirit reaches out. As its fingers close around the flower, color floods into the petals — brighter than before. The spirit smiles. A single tear rolls down its face and crystallizes in the air before falling into your palm. 'Thank you,' it whispers, and fades into the moonlight.

The spirit reaches out. As its fingers close around the flower, color floods into the petals — brighter than before. The spirit smiles. A single tear rolls down its face and crystallizes in the air before falling into your palm. 'Thank you,' it whispers, and fades into the moonlight.
The spirit looks at the apple and shakes its head slowly. 'Something growing. Not something harvested.'
The spirit eyes the banana with what might be amusement. 'That is... not what I meant.'
You try to count them. You lose track after three hundred. Each one is scratched deep into the stone. Days? Years? Whatever this was, they were here a long time.
You try to count them. You lose track after three hundred. Each one is scratched deep into the stone.
It's brighter than any star you've seen. It pulses faintly, like a heartbeat.
It's brighter than any star you've seen. It pulses faintly, like a heartbeat.
You descend the crumbling staircase back to the clearing.
You descend the crumbling staircase back to the clearing.
You descend the crumbling staircase back to the clearing.
You descend the crumbling staircase back to the clearing.
You're in an old watchtower. Try: look around, talk to the spirit, inspect things, give items.
A watchtower overlooking Spookytown. A spirit lingers here.
A watchtower overlooking Spookytown. The spirit has moved on. Only moonlight remains.
The lantern's flame brightens. For a moment, the spirit seems more solid — you can almost make out features. Then it flickers back to translucence.
The compass needle spins wildly, then points straight down. The void ripples. A path of light unfurls beneath your feet, leading somewhere new. You follow it.
The needle is frantic. It wants to show you something.

Light. Actual, warm light. You're standing on a glass plain that stretches to the horizon. The sky is pale gold. In the distance, something tall and bright shimmers — a city, maybe. Or a memory of one. The compass needle has gone still. It points forward.
The sky is the color of late afternoon, forever. No sun, but light comes from everywhere.
Glass, or something like it. Beneath it, clouds drift slowly. You're standing on the sky.
You walk toward the shimmering city. With each step, the glass beneath you chimes softly. The city grows larger, but never closer. Maybe that's the point. You keep walking anyway.
Towers of light, shifting and reforming. It looks like every place you've ever wanted to be, layered on top of itself. It's beautiful and impossible.
Towers of light, shifting and reforming. It looks like every place you've ever wanted to be, layered on top of itself. It's beautiful and impossible.
The needle spins gently, then points at your chest. Oh. It was never pointing to a place.
Beyond. Past the void, past the dark. Somewhere that feels like it was waiting for you.
You're beyond the void. Look around. Walk forward. Use what you have.
The needle points at you. Steady as a heartbeat.
CRACK. The whole room groans. A jagged split runs across the floor from wall to wall.
CRACK. The whole room groans. A jagged split runs across the floor from wall to wall.
CRACK. The impact sends a shudder through everything. The floor splits wide.
CRACK. The whole room groans. A jagged split runs across the floor from wall to wall.
CRACK. The whole room groans. A jagged split runs across the floor from wall to wall.
CRACK. The impact sends a shudder through everything. The floor splits wide.
The floor is barely holding together. One more stomp and you're going through.
The floor is barely holding together. One more stomp and you're going through.
The floor is barely holding together. One more stomp and you're going through.
The floor is barely holding together. One more stomp and you're going through.
A crack runs along the left side of the floor. The wood is splintering.
A crack runs along the right side of the floor. The wood is splintering.
A gaping crack runs across the entire floor. Cold air rises from the darkness below. One good stomp and you'd fall right through.
A gaping crack runs across the entire floor. Cold air rises from the darkness below.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.
You bring your foot down hard. The wood gives way and you tumble through darkness, landing in a heap on cold stone. Dust rains down from above.\nYou're in some kind of cellar.

A stone cellar. Collapsed shelving lines one wall, broken bottles glinting in the dim light. A grandfather clock stands against the far wall, still ticking. A narrow passage leads into darkness to the east. Above, you can see the hole you fell through.
A stone cellar. Broken glass everywhere. The grandfather clock is open and empty. A narrow passage leads east into darkness.
Collapsed shelving. Broken bottles. Something glints among the glass.
A grandfather clock. It shouldn't still be ticking, but it is.
The grandfather clock, its face hanging open. Empty inside.
The hole you fell through. The room above is too high to reach.
Cold stone floor. A drain in one corner, water trickling through it.
A grandfather clock, impossibly old. The pendulum swings. The face reads a time that can't be right. There's a small keyhole beneath the face.
The clock face hangs open. Inside, the mechanism is a nest of gears — except for one conspicuous gap where a gear is missing. No, wait. You already took it.
The glass over the face is cracked. Behind it, you can hear gears turning. The keyhole is too small for any key you've seen.
You find a winding stem on the side and turn it. The ticking accelerates. The hands spin. The glass cracks further. The face pops open with a click.
You try to pry it open. It won't budge. Maybe try winding it first.
Already open. The mechanism is exposed — an intricate nest of gears with one conspicuous gap.
You carefully extract the brass gear. It's warm to the touch, still humming faintly. The clock stops ticking.
You carefully extract the brass gear. It's warm to the touch, still humming faintly. The clock stops ticking.
Rotten wood, collapsed inward. Among the broken bottles, a long shard of green glass catches the light. Sharp enough to cut.
Mostly ruined. Wine bottles, all smashed. One shard stands out — long, green, wickedly sharp.
You wrap the base in a scrap of cloth and pick it up carefully. It's surprisingly sturdy.
You wrap the base in a scrap of cloth and pick it up carefully. It's surprisingly sturdy.
Water trickles into a grate in the floor. Something glints below the grate — wedged between the bars.
You work your fingers through the grate and fish out... a bent coin. Someone dropped this a long time ago.
Something metallic, wedged between the bars.
You squeeze through the narrow passage. The air gets cooler as the cellar falls behind you.
You squeeze through the narrow passage into the tunnels.
The hole is too high to reach. You're not getting back up that way.
You jump for the edge of the hole. Not even close. The ceiling is too high.
The clock ticks. Water drips somewhere. From the eastern passage, a faint echo — could be wind, could be something else.
Water drips somewhere. From the eastern passage, a faint echo — could be wind, could be something else.

A junction of rough-hewn stone corridors. Glowing mushrooms cling to the walls in clusters, casting everything in pale blue. Three paths branch off: a breeze from the north, dripping water to the south, and the cellar passage behind you to the west.
The northern passage. A faint breeze carries the smell of green things.
The southern passage. You can hear water dripping steadily.
Low ceiling, rough stone. The mushrooms are brighter here, clustered around the junction.
Damp stone floor, smooth from running water long ago.
Bioluminescent. They pulse faintly, like breathing. One cluster near the floor looks different from the others — rounder, with a faint sweet smell.
They glow pale blue. Beautiful, in an unsettling way.
You bite into one. It tastes like starlight and regret. For a moment, you can hear the mushrooms humming. Then it passes. You feel... fine? Probably fine.
It crumbles the moment you pull it from the wall. The glow fades instantly.
Chisel marks. Someone carved these tunnels deliberately. Among the tool marks, a small symbol repeats: a circle with a line through it.
Rough stone, glowing mushrooms. Chisel marks suggesting these were carved by hand.
You follow the breeze. The tunnel narrows, then opens into light.
The air gets warmer. The tunnel narrows, then opens into light.
You follow the northern passage toward the smell of green things.
You follow the sound of dripping water deeper underground.
The sound of water grows louder as you descend.
You follow the southern passage toward the water.
You head back through the narrow passage to the cellar.
You retrace your steps to the cellar.
You head back to the cellar.
Wind from the north. Water from the south. Silence from the west.
An underground reservoir. Still black water fills a wide stone basin. A heavy chain hangs from the ceiling into the water. The chamber echoes. The tunnel back north is the only way out.
The drained reservoir. The basin is empty, revealing a mosaic on the floor and a corroded lockbox in the corner. The chain hangs slack from the ceiling.
Black water. You can't see the bottom.
The basin floor is covered in a mosaic of colored tiles. A corroded lockbox sits in the corner.
The chain disappears into a mechanism in the ceiling. Some kind of sluice gate.
Perfectly still and perfectly black. Occasionally, something pale moves beneath the surface. Blind fish, maybe.
You lean close. Pale shapes drift in the dark — eyeless fish, translucent as ghosts.
You cup some in your hands. It's ice cold and tastes of stone. The fish scatter at your touch.
Thick iron links, rusted but solid. It connects to something in the ceiling — a sluice gate, maybe. Worth pulling.

You haul on the chain. Something groans overhead. A grinding sound, then a rush — water begins pouring out through a drain in the far wall. Slowly, the basin empties. The fish thrash, then slip away through the drain.\nThe floor of the reservoir is revealed.
The chain hangs slack. The reservoir is already drained.

You haul on the chain. Something groans overhead. A grinding sound, then a rush — water begins pouring out through a drain in the far wall. Slowly, the basin empties.\nThe floor of the reservoir is revealed.

Tiny colored tiles form a map. You recognize a room, a plaza, a dark town. But there are places marked that you haven't seen — a garden, a tower, passages underground. At the center, a compass rose. Beneath it, in faded letters: ALL PATHS LEAD INWARD.
A map of... everything? Places you've been and places you haven't. It's beautiful and unsettling.
Corroded green metal, sealed shut. No keyhole — just corrosion fusing it closed. You'd need something sharp to pry it open.
A metal box, crusted shut with age.
You pull at the lid. The corrosion holds fast. You'd need something sharp to pry it.

You work the glass shard under the lid and lever it up. The corrosion cracks. Inside: a small round mirror, perfectly intact. Its surface doesn't show your reflection — it shows light. Pure, concentrated light, like a piece of trapped noon.
With what? You'd need something sharp.
You work the glass shard under the lid. The corrosion cracks. Inside: a small round mirror that doesn't show your reflection — it shows light. Pure, trapped light.
Blind cave fish, pale as paper. They drift in slow circles, unbothered by your presence.
You try. They slip through your fingers like cold silk.
You head back through the passage to the tunnel junction.
You head back to the tunnels.
You return to the tunnel junction.

An overgrown walled garden, open to the sky. Stone paths disappear under moss and wildflowers. An ancient fountain trickles at the center near a sundial. To the west, an iron gate leads to what looks like a plaza. To the east, a thorny hedge archway opens onto a dark path. Against the south wall, a glass greenhouse, fogged and locked. Behind you, the tunnel entrance.
The walled garden. Fountain, sundial, wildflowers. The greenhouse door stands open now. Gate west to the plaza. Thorny archway east to the dark path. Tunnel south.
The iron gate to the west. Through the bars, you can see an open plaza.
The thorny hedge archway. Beyond it, darkness.
Open sky. After the tunnels, it feels impossibly wide.
Moss-covered flagstones. Tiny flowers growing between the cracks.

Carved from a single block of stone, cracked and green with age. Water still trickles from somewhere — a thin stream down the basin. At the bottom, a few coins, too deep to reach. Wishes that never came true.
An ancient stone fountain, barely flowing. Coins at the bottom.
The coin sinks slowly. For a moment, all the coins at the bottom glow — then fade. You feel lighter.
The water is cool and sweet, with a faint mineral taste. It's the best water you've ever had.
Brass, gone green. The gnomon casts a shadow that doesn't match the sun. Engraved around the edge: THE HOURS ARE NOT WHAT THEY SEEM. COME BACK WHEN THE SHADOW POINTS NOWHERE.
A brass sundial. The shadow falls on a symbol you don't recognize.
You trace your finger along the engraved hours. The shadow shivers, then settles. Whatever this means, you haven't figured it out yet.
Glass panels, most intact, fogged from inside. The door has a mechanism — not a lock exactly, more like a clockwork latch. A gear-shaped socket sits empty at its center.
A glass greenhouse against the south wall. The door mechanism has an empty gear-shaped socket.
The clockwork latch won't budge. There's a gear-shaped socket that needs filling.
Already open. The door stands ajar, warm air spilling out.
The gear slides into the socket with a satisfying click. The mechanism whirs to life — gears turning gears turning gears. The latch releases. The door swings open, and warm humid air spills out.
The gear slides into the socket. The mechanism whirs to life. The greenhouse door swings open.

You step inside. It's warm and close, like being inside a breath. A single tree stands in the center, withered but alive. Its branches reach toward the glass ceiling. Soil, cracked and dry. The tree needs something — light, real light, not the grey filter through fogged glass.
The door is locked. A gear-shaped socket sits empty in the clockwork latch.
The tree stands tall now, golden fruit heavy on its branches. Light pours from the mirror embedded in the soil, refracting through the glass walls.
Withered but not dead. The bark is warm to the touch. It's waiting for something. Light, maybe. Real light.
Bare branches, cracked soil. Alive, barely.
Golden fruit hangs from every branch. The tree hums with warmth. You did this.

You set the mirror in the soil at the tree's base. It catches what little light filters through the fogged glass and — amplifies it. Concentrates it. A beam of pure noon hits the trunk.\nThe tree shudders. Bark splits and heals in the same instant. Leaves unfurl, impossibly green. Then flowers. Then fruit — golden, luminous, warm.\nThe greenhouse fills with light.

You angle the mirror toward the tree. A beam of concentrated light strikes the trunk.\nThe tree shudders. Bark splits and heals. Leaves unfurl. Flowers bloom. Then fruit — golden and warm.\nThe greenhouse fills with light.
The mirror shows light, not reflections. But there's nothing here that needs it.
You reach up and twist one free. It's warm in your hands, heavier than it looks, and it pulses faintly — like a heartbeat.
You reach up and twist one free. It's warm in your hands, heavier than it looks, and it pulses faintly — like a heartbeat.
You reach up and twist one free. It's warm, heavier than it looks, pulsing faintly.
You push through the iron gate. It groans open, and you step into a familiar open plaza.
You push through the iron gate into the plaza.
The gate groans open. The plaza stretches out before you.
You duck through the thorny archway. Branches scrape your shoulders. The darkness beyond swallows you, then spits you out into a clearing.
You push through the thorny hedge. It opens onto a dark clearing.
Thorns catch your clothes as you push through. Then you're out — into darkness.
You duck back into the tunnel entrance.
You head back into the tunnels.
You head back into the tunnels.
Birdsong. The fountain. Wind through the hedge. It's peaceful here.
Sweet and wild. Nothing cultivated about this garden anymore. It belongs to itself.
They pause their singing to look at you. Then resume, slightly louder, as if performing.
Wrought iron, partially overgrown with vines. Through the bars, you can see an open plaza with a signpost.
Dense and thorny, but there's a gap — an archway, almost deliberate. Beyond it, darkness and the faint smell of smoke.
You slip through a vine-covered gate you hadn't noticed before. It opens into a walled garden.
Partially hidden by vines. Through the bars, you can see an overgrown garden with a fountain.
A vine-covered iron gate in the wall. It looks like it leads somewhere green.
You push through the thorny hedge. Branches part reluctantly, then you stumble into green light and birdsong.

The spirit reaches out. As its fingers close around the apple, the gold deepens to amber, then to sunset red. Light pours between its fingers.\nThe spirit smiles — wider than before, full of warmth. A single tear rolls down its face and crystallizes before falling into your palm.\n'Where did you find this?' it whispers. 'It tastes like a whole life.' And it fades into the light.

The spirit reaches out. As its fingers close around the apple, light pours between them.\nIt smiles. A tear crystallizes and falls into your palm.\n'Where did you find this?' it whispers. And it fades into the light.
The spirit takes the golden apple. Light pours between its fingers. It smiles, weeps a single crystallized tear into your palm, and fades into warmth.
A flat stone near the fountain, covered in scratched names. Some are old enough to be illegible. There's space to add yours.
You scratch your name into the stone with a sharp edge. It joins dozens of others. Some you recognize. Some you don't. How long has this garden been here?
Scratch marks everywhere. Some are tally marks. Some are words: PULL THE CHAIN. And below that, in different handwriting: DON'T. And below that: I DID. IT WAS WORTH IT.

The garden glows under a low sun. Long shadows stretch from the fountain. Fireflies are beginning to appear among the wildflowers. The greenhouse glass catches the last light like amber. Gate west. Hedge east. Tunnel south.

Moonlight fills the garden. Fireflies drift everywhere, thick as snow. The fountain catches starlight. The sundial's shadow has vanished entirely. Everything is silver and alive. Gate west. Hedge east. Tunnel south.

First light. Dew on everything. The garden holds its breath. A single bird begins to sing, then another, then the whole garden erupts. The sundial casts its first shadow of the day. Gate west. Hedge east. Tunnel south.
The shadow has vanished. The brass surface reflects moonlight. The engraved text seems to shift: COME BACK WHEN THE SHADOW POINTS NOWHERE. This is that moment.

You place your hand on the brass plate where the shadow should be. It's warm — warmer than it should be at night. Something clicks beneath the surface. A tiny drawer slides out of the base: inside, a single perfect coin.
The little drawer is already open and empty.
The hands point to... now? That can't be right. The face reads the current hour perfectly. How?
Dawn reaches even here, barely. The darkness thins to grey. For a moment, the hut almost looks cozy. The iron gate, the tower, the dark path — all softened by the early light. It won't last.
Someone has already pulled the chain. The reservoir is drained. You can see the mosaic floor and a lockbox in the corner.
You scratch your name into the stone. It joins others — some fresh, some ancient. A community of strangers who all found this garden.
A flat stone near the fountain. Fresh names scratched alongside ancient ones. You're not the first to find this place. You won't be the last.
You gather scattered sticks and strike a spark. A small fire catches, flickering bravely against the darkness. It won't last forever, but it's here now.
Someone lit a fire here. It crackles softly, pushing back the dark. You're not alone in this.
Warm. Recent. Someone else passed through here and thought to leave a light.
You sit. The warmth is real. For a moment, Spookytown feels almost bearable.
You scratch a message into the wood. Maybe someone will read it. Maybe it'll outlast you.

The signpost has three arms pointing in different directions: Umbra Road (but the path is closed), Spookytown, and E-b--- (the rest is chipped off). Below, someone has scratched a fresh message into the wood.
You place something small at the base of the tower. It feels right. Maybe the next visitor will understand.
Someone left something at the base of the tower. A small gesture, deliberately placed. You're not the first to feel something here.
You scratch an arrow into the stone, pointing toward the garden. Maybe it'll help the next person through.
Chisel marks. Someone carved these tunnels deliberately. Among the tool marks — a fresh arrow, scratched recently, pointing north toward the garden.
